content.text: A GenAI Center of Excellence (CoE) without a formal charter is a collection of enthusiasts, not an institutional function. The charter defines
what the CoE is authorized to do

, who it answers to, how it makes decisions, and what boundaries it may not cross. Without that contract, business units ignore it, budgets evaporate at the first reorg, and the CoE degrades into an advisory body that issues recommendations no one reads.

attrs.alt: GenAI CoE governance structure: Executive Steering Committee at top, CoE core in center, embedded AI guilds in each business unit below, and shared platform services at the bottom

content.text: A CoE charter is a formal document — often a one-page mandate ratified by the Executive Steering Committee — that covers six elements:
Prevents scope creep; provides a north-star for trade-off decisions
What decisions can the CoE make unilaterally vs. only recommend?
Removes ambiguity about authority; prevents BU conflicts
Who pays for the CoE and shared infrastructure?
Ensures the CoE survives budget cycles
Who are the permanent CoE members vs. rotating BU liaisons?
How often does the CoE make decisions and with whom?
When is the CoE's mandate reviewed?
Prevents bureaucratic calcification; ties mandate to business results

The GenAI CoE exists to accelerate responsible AI adoption across the enterprise by providing shared platforms, technical standards, risk guardrails, and enablement programs. It is the accountable function for AI governance and the authoritative body on model usage policy. It does not own product roadmaps — it enables product teams to build safely and at speed.
(does not own product roadmaps). The exclusion clause is as important as the inclusions — without it, the CoE becomes a bottleneck or a threat to BU autonomy.
Not all CoE decisions carry the same weight. Conflating authority and advisory influence is the fastest path to CoE irrelevance. Define three tiers:
Decisions the CoE makes unilaterally; BUs must comply
Approved model list, data classification rules, prompt injection guardrails, GDPR consent gates
BU proposals that require CoE sign-off before deployment
New third-party LLM integration, handling PII in prompts, using models for high-stakes decisions
CoE provides guidance; BUs decide autonomously
Prompt design patterns, retrieval architecture, evaluation approach, model fine-tuning strategy
Document each in the charter. Publishing this list to all engineering teams removes the most common friction: BUs not knowing whether they need CoE approval or not.
A RACI matrix makes decision rights explicit. The following covers the decisions most CoEs fight over:
Approve new foundation model for production
Set rate limits and cost controls
Manage model registry and version policy
RACI key: R = Responsible (does the work), A = Accountable (owns the outcome), C = Consulted (input required before decision), I = Informed (notified after decision).

: Legal and the AI Ethics Lead are both Responsible (they both do the review work), while the Exec Steering Committee is Accountable. This prevents the CoE from rubber-stamping use cases that carry regulatory or reputational risk.
: a central CoE (the hub) provides shared capabilities and binding standards, while each business unit maintains an
(the spoke) that translates CoE standards into BU-specific implementations.
Maintain the approved model registry and evaluate new foundation models
Own the shared guardrail gateway (PII detection, toxicity filters, content policy enforcement)
Run the enterprise LLMOps platform (experiment tracking, prompt versioning, deployment pipelines)
Publish and enforce the AI acceptable-use policy
Operate the AI risk register and escalation process
Deliver the AI enablement program (training, certifications, internal community)
Report CoE metrics to the Exec Steering Committee quarterly
Translate CoE standards into BU-specific tooling and patterns
Act as the CoE's point of contact in the BU for intake and escalations
Participate in the CoE governance cadence (bi-weekly guild sync)
Review BU use cases against Tier 2 gates before submitting to CoE
Surface BU-specific requirements back to the CoE platform roadmap
Guild leads attend the bi-weekly CoE operating cadence. This is the mechanism that prevents the hub from becoming disconnected from ground truth.
Below is a condensed charter template you can adapt. In practice, the full charter is 3–5 pages; this is the operative section that gets shared with all engineering and product leaders:

# GenAI Center of Excellence — Charter v1.0
Ratified by: Executive Steering Committee
Effective date: [date]
Review date: [date + 12 months]

## Mission
Accelerate responsible GenAI adoption across [Company] by providing shared
platforms, technical standards, risk guardrails, and enablement programs.

## Mandate (what the CoE is authorized to do)
- Maintain and enforce the AI acceptable-use policy (Tier 1 — binding)
- Operate the approved model registry; no unapproved model may serve production traffic
- Define and enforce prompt injection and PII guardrails on all customer-facing LLM endpoints
- Gate high-risk AI use cases (Tier 2 — requires CoE sign-off)
- Publish advisory guidance on architecture, prompting, and evaluation (Tier 3)

## Out of scope
- Product roadmap ownership (owned by respective product BUs)
- Data pipeline engineering outside AI inference paths
- Individual ML model training for non-GenAI use cases

## Governance bodies
- Executive Steering Committee: CTO, CDO, CISO, CFO — meets quarterly
- CoE Operating Board: CoE Lead + all guild leads — meets bi-weekly
- AI Risk Review Panel: CoE Lead + AI Ethics + Legal — convenes on demand for Tier 2 gates

## Funding
CoE core team and shared platform costs funded via central IT budget.
Embedded guild members funded by respective BU headcount.
Platform consumption billed back to BUs via internal showback model.

## Success metrics (reviewed quarterly)
- Time-to-first-production (new use case): target < 8 weeks
- Tier 2 gate review SLA: target 100% completed within 5 business days
- Policy violation rate: target < 2% of monthly API calls
- AI enablement coverage: target 80% of AI practitioners certified within 12 months
The CoE's funding model determines its independence and longevity. Three patterns:
CTO/CDO budget funds the CoE fully
First target in budget cuts; perceived as overhead
Year 1 — when the CoE is establishing credibility
BUs pay for shared platform usage via internal chargeback
BUs may under-use platform to avoid cost
Year 2+ — when platform provides measurable value
Each BU funds one embedded guild member who also contributes to CoE
Complexity; guild members face conflicting priorities
Large, federated organizations with strong BU autonomy
Most mature enterprise CoEs use a
hybrid: central budget for the CoE core team and guardrail gateway
(model inference costs, storage, evaluation runs). This keeps the CoE stable while giving BUs cost-visibility and incentive to optimize.

# Monthly showback report per business unit
def compute_bu_showback(bu_name: str, usage_records: list[dict]) -> dict:
    """
    usage_records: list of {model, tokens_in, tokens_out, eval_runs, storage_gb}
    Rates are internal transfer prices, not actual vendor costs.
    """
    RATES = {
        "gpt-4o":       {"input": 0.000005, "output": 0.000015},   # per token
        "claude-3-5":   {"input": 0.000003, "output": 0.000015},
        "embedding":    {"input": 0.0000001, "output": 0.0},
    }
    EVAL_RUN_COST = 0.50       # internal rate per evaluation run
    STORAGE_COST_PER_GB = 0.02 # per GB per month

    inference_cost = 0.0
    for record in usage_records:
        rate = RATES.get(record["model"], {"input": 0.0, "output": 0.0})
        inference_cost += (
            record["tokens_in"] * rate["input"] +
            record["tokens_out"] * rate["output"]
        )

    eval_cost = sum(r.get("eval_runs", 0) for r in usage_records) * EVAL_RUN_COST
    storage_cost = sum(r.get("storage_gb", 0) for r in usage_records) * STORAGE_COST_PER_GB

    return {
        "business_unit": bu_name,
        "inference_usd": round(inference_cost, 2),
        "evaluation_usd": round(eval_cost, 2),
        "storage_usd": round(storage_cost, 2),
        "total_usd": round(inference_cost + eval_cost + storage_cost, 2),
    }

# Example
records = [
    {"model": "gpt-4o",     "tokens_in": 1_200_000, "tokens_out": 400_000, "eval_runs": 80, "storage_gb": 5},
    {"model": "claude-3-5", "tokens_in": 800_000,   "tokens_out": 300_000, "eval_runs": 40, "storage_gb": 2},
    {"model": "embedding",  "tokens_in": 5_000_000, "tokens_out": 0,       "eval_runs": 0,  "storage_gb": 10},
]
print(compute_bu_showback("engineering", records))
# {'business_unit': 'engineering', 'inference_usd': 16.1, 'evaluation_usd': 60.0,
#  'storage_usd': 0.34, 'total_usd': 76.44}
Every BU wishing to build a GenAI feature goes through a lightweight intake process. This is not bureaucracy — it is the mechanism that catches compliance risks before they reach production:
BU submits a use-case intake form (5 fields: description, data classification, user population, output action, estimated monthly calls)
CoE program manager triages: is this Tier 2 (requires gate review) or Tier 3 (advisory only)?
For Tier 2: AI Risk Review Panel convenes within 5 business days; outputs a risk memo with approval, conditional approval, or rejection
For Tier 3: CoE architect assigns a technical advisor; BU proceeds autonomously with advisory support
All approved use cases are logged in the AI use-case registry (single source of truth for the risk register and audit trail)
A use case triggers Tier 2 review if it meets
Output is used to make or directly influence a consequential decision for an individual (credit, employment, healthcare, legal)
Prompts or context will include PII, PHI, or confidential financial data
The system uses a model not yet in the approved model registry
The system interacts directly with external users (customers, patients, citizens) in an autonomous or semi-autonomous capacity
Estimated monthly cost exceeds the BU's pre-approved AI spend threshold
A CoE that tries to hire a generic "AI team" will fail at the first cross-functional conflict. Each role has a distinct accountability that must be non-overlapping:
Overall CoE P&L, exec relationship, charter stewardship
Technical standards, model evaluation, platform architecture
Responsible AI framework, bias audits, high-risk use-case review
Shared deployment pipeline, model registry, observability
Intake process, governance cadence, metrics, charter review
BU-specific AI adoption, CoE liaison, Tier 2 pre-screening
BU Engineering Lead (dotted to CoE Lead)
The dotted-line reporting for guild leads is deliberate: they are funded by the BU but governed by the CoE. This creates
that, if managed well, keeps both sides honest — the CoE hears ground-truth requirements from BUs, and BUs cannot silently bypass governance.
What the CoE Should NOT Staff
Application engineers building BU-specific GenAI features (BU responsibility)
Data engineers building the data pipelines that feed GenAI systems (Data Platform team responsibility)
Security red-team for GenAI (Security team responsibility, with CoE as stakeholder)
Legal counsel for AI regulation (Legal's responsibility, with CoE as the technical subject-matter expert)
Overstaffing these roles inside the CoE is the surest way to generate resentment from adjacent functions and to balloon the CoE's cost beyond what executive sponsors will defend.

The CoE owns a centralized AI risk register — a living document that tracks every approved AI use case, its risk classification, its current status, and its open remediation items. This is the artifact auditors ask for first, and the primary input to the quarterly governance report.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional

class RiskTier(Enum):
    LOW    = "low"     # Tier 3 advisory only
    MEDIUM = "medium"  # Tier 2 gate required
    HIGH   = "high"    # Tier 2 gate + quarterly re-review
    CRITICAL = "critical"  # Tier 2 gate + exec sign-off + continuous monitoring

class UseCase Status(Enum):
    INTAKE   = "intake"
    REVIEW   = "review"      # In Tier 2 gate process
    APPROVED = "approved"
    DEPLOYED = "deployed"
    PAUSED   = "paused"      # Deployed but suspended pending remediation
    RETIRED  = "retired"

@dataclass
class AIRiskRecord:
    use_case_id: str          # e.g. "UC-2024-047"
    title: str
    business_unit: str
    owner: str                # BU product owner
    guild_lead: str           # Embedded guild lead
    risk_tier: RiskTier
    status: UseCase Status
    data_classification: str  # public / internal / confidential / restricted
    pii_in_prompts: bool
    models_used: list[str]
    monthly_call_estimate: int
    approval_date: Optional[date] = None
    next_review_date: Optional[date] = None
    open_findings: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    notes: str = ""
The register drives three downstream processes: the
to the Exec Steering Committee (how many use cases at each risk tier, how many open findings), the
(which use cases have drift from original approval scope), and the
(which use cases to suspend first if a model provider suffers a breach).

Encoding this as a function — rather than a spreadsheet checkbox — means the same logic runs at intake time, at the quarterly re-review, and in any CI/CD gate that validates use-case metadata against the registry.
BUs ignore CoE guidance; no consequences for non-compliance
Require sign-off from CTO + CDO before any CoE enforcement activity
BUs see CoE as a threat to their autonomy; political resistance
Explicitly list what the CoE does NOT own in the charter
BUs route around the CoE to avoid delay
Define a 5-day SLA; auto-approve if no response within SLA
CoE funded entirely by central IT
CoE priorities drift from BU needs; viewed as overhead
Add BU representatives to governance; move to showback model by Year 2
Charter becomes outdated; CoE mandate grows without accountability
Review charter and metrics annually; tie renewal to business outcomes
Guild leads have no protected time
Guild members are 100% tasked on BU work; CoE gets no input
Charter must specify guild leads allocate minimum 20% time to CoE activities

A CoE charter is the organizational contract that transforms an AI interest group into an accountable governance function. The mandatory elements are: mission statement with explicit exclusions, a three-tier mandate (binding / gating / advisory), a RACI for every contested decision, a funded hub-and-spoke structure with embedded guild leads, and an intake process with defined SLAs. Without the charter, the CoE has no authority; without the exclusions, it has no allies.
Define three mandate tiers — binding (CoE decides), gating (CoE approves), advisory (BU decides) — and publish them to all engineering teams
Specify decision rights with a RACI covering model approval, policy ownership, high-risk use-case gating, and regulatory response
Fund the CoE core centrally in Year 1; move platform consumption to internal showback by Year 2
Set a 5-business-day SLA for Tier 2 gate reviews — credibility depends on speed as much as rigor
Include a sunset clause: review the charter and CoE metrics annually; tie mandate renewal to business outcomes
